nativeRange.habitat: Mixed coral and rubble areas of both lagoon and seaward reefs at depths of roughly 4–55 m, often near caves and overhangs.

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on benthic algae in the wild but is also documented taking microcrustaceans and detritus. In captivity, has been reported to accept Spirulina, marine algae sheets, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis and frozen shrimp, and other meaty fare. Continuous browsing on live rock is generally considered beneficial, so multiple small feedings per day tend to be recommended over a single large meal.

avoidWith: other dwarf angelfish in small tanks
conspecifics outside of an established harem
very timid or slow feeders that may be outcompeted

summary: The largest of the Centropyge dwarf angelfishes, a Western Pacific reef species generally considered intermediate to keep. Best suited to a mature, well-aquascaped reef or FOWLR aquarium with plenty of live rock to graze on.
careNotes:
The Tibicen (also commonly sold as Keyhole or Melas) Angelfish is the largest member of the Centropyge genus, with adults commonly reaching 14–19 cm — substantially larger than most dwarf angelfish. Sources generally advise housing in a tank with both ample live rock for grazing and open swimming space; the recorded 400 L minimum reflects this size and activity level rather than a strict average of the three figures encountered (70 gal/265 L from LiveAquaria, 75 gal/284 L from a hobby summary, 400 L from ReefApp). Aquarists keeping a single specimen in a smaller volume have been reported, but the larger figure is generally considered more appropriate for long-term keeping of an adult.

This species is commonly considered 'reef safe with caution'; nipping at large-polyp stony corals, soft coral polyps, zoanthids, and clam mantles has been reported. ReefApp considers it not reliably reef-safe; LiveAquaria lists it as reef-safe with caution. Behavior in a given tank tends to be individual-specific, and the risk in a prized SPS-dominated reef is generally considered non-trivial.

In the wild, Centropyge tibicen forms harems of 3–7 individuals with one dominant male. Like other Centropyge angelfish, it is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite; the dominant individual transitions to male, with the sex reversal reported to complete in roughly 29–35 days. No reliable external sex differentiation is documented in the aquarium trade.

Cross-source disagreement: LiveAquaria lists adult size at 8 inches (~20 cm) while FishBase and ReefApp give 19 cm — these agree closely and a 14–19 cm range was recorded. Temperature ranges differ: LiveAquaria 22.2–25.6°C, FishBase 24.5–29°C, third hobby source 22.2–25.6°C; the recorded 23–27°C is the approximate average across the three sources. The aquarium hobby wiki (theaquariumwiki.com) returned an access-denied page on this fetch and was not used.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely achieved and generally considered very difficult. No reliable external sex differentiation has been documented. The species is protogynous, with sex change reported to take roughly 29–35 days. Wild harems are not readily replicated in home aquariums.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes), max size (19.0 cm TL), temperature range (24.5–29°C), distribution (Christmas Island east to Fiji, southern Japan south to Lord Howe Island), depth range (4–55 m), and harem/protogynous biology. Temperature differs from LiveAquaria; averaged for the recorded 23–27°C range.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 19 cm. Reports minimum tank volume 400 L vs LiveAquaria's 70 gal (265 L); the larger figure was selected for the recorded minimum given the species' adult size and activity. Considers the species not reliably reef-safe, in tension with LiveAquaria's 'with caution'.

additional.name: The Aquarium Wiki (via web search excerpt)
additional.notes: Direct fetch was blocked by access-denied page (Anubis); only used the search-result excerpt, which gave 75 gal (~284 L) minimum, 22.2–25.6°C, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12. Cross-checked water parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding rarely achieved. Protogynous; sex reversal reported to take 29–35 days. No external sex differentiation known.
